bins.name: Raise Threshold to 30 MW
bins.desc: PSEG argues the proposed 20 MW threshold is too low and would inadvertently cover distribution-level commercial loads outside FERC jurisdiction. It recommends increasing the threshold to at least 30 MW, noting the average U.S. data center already exceeds 40 MW.
quotes: the Commission should increase the 20 MW threshold to 30 MW or greater to avoid pulling in non-data center customers or other commercial customers who were not intended to be covered by the ANOPR.

bins.name: Charge Based on Actual Use
bins.desc: PSEG grounds its transmission pricing position in the Commission's cost causation authority, arguing that rates must reflect each customer's actual use of the interstate transmission system.
quotes: transmission charges should be based on actual usage of the transmission system, guided by well-established cost causation principles.

bins.name: Keep Resource Adequacy RTO-Wide
bins.desc: PSEG opposes folding resource adequacy analysis into individual co-location interconnection studies. It argues resource adequacy is a system-wide concern that RTOs should address at the regional level, not through project-specific RMR studies.
quotes: it would be inappropriate to consider resource adequacy issues in the study process for co-located load/hybrid arrangements.

bins.name: Flexible Configuration Choice
bins.desc: PSEG supports giving large loads the freedom to choose front-of-meter or co-location configurations without regulatory barriers, consistent with the Administration's AI development goals.
quotes: the Commission should provide data centers with the flexibility and optionality to select a configuration that best fits their particular circumstances and needs, including in front of the meter connections or co-location/behind the meter configurations.

bins.name: No RMR Study for Co-Located Loads
bins.desc: PSEG opposes applying SSR/RMR-type studies to co-located load arrangements. It contends such studies are designed for generator retirement decisions, not co-location, and applying them here would be unduly discriminatory under the FPA.
quotes: applying such a study to co-located load arrangement would be unduly discriminatory under the FPA because it would treat co-located load arrangements differently than loads seeking to interconnect on a front of the meter basis.
layering on an RMR study that examines resource adequacy issues is unnecessary, inefficient, unduly discriminatory, and not the correct legal or practical fit for these arrangements.

bins.name: Fast-Track State-Sponsored Generation
bins.desc: PSEG calls for an expedited interconnection track specifically for state-sponsored generation to address the growing supply gap in PJM and other regions before large load growth fully materializes.
quotes: there is an urgent need to create an expedited interconnection track to bring state-sponsored resources online faster.

bins.name: Differentiated Upgrade Cost Assignment
bins.desc: PSEG proposes a two-track approach: front-of-meter upgrade costs should be rolled into transmission rates and shared by all customers (because they provide system-wide benefit), while upgrade costs for co-located arrangements should be directly assigned to those arrangements (because they do not use the transmission system).
quotes: network upgrades to accommodate load interconnections in front of the meter should be allocated to all transmission customers, including large load additions.
network upgrades required by co-location load arrangements should continue to be directly assigned to the co-location arrangements.

bins.name: Expedite Interconnection Process
bins.desc: PSEG supports a timely, standardized study process for large load interconnections and wants the Commission to act quickly. It also requests a fast-track study path for state-sponsored generation to close the resource adequacy gap.
quotes: the time has come for the Commission to act expeditiously by establishing rules that will provide clarity and finality.
there is an urgent need to create an expedited interconnection track to bring state-sponsored resources online faster.
layering on an RMR study that examines resource adequacy issues is unnecessary, inefficient, unduly discriminatory, and not the correct legal or practical fit for these arrangements.

bins.name: Cost Causation as Rate Foundation
bins.desc: PSEG argues that all transmission charges for large loads must be grounded in cost causation, meaning charges must reflect actual system use. Gross-load billing for co-located arrangements would violate this principle and harm existing behind-the-meter generation customers.
quotes: large load connections should recognize and prioritize affordability for customers through efficient planning and use of the transmission system.
transmission charges should be based on actual usage of the transmission system, guided by well-established cost causation principles.

a transmission pricing methodology for a co-located arrangement that is based on gross load without any consideration for actual usage of the transmission system or the impact or use of the transmission system would clearly fail to comply with cost causation principles.
co-located load is using the transmission system in the same manner as BTMG customers and there would be no justification to treat behind the meter load different from BTMG.
network upgrades to accommodate load interconnections in front of the meter should be allocated to all transmission customers, including large load additions.
network upgrades required by co-location load arrangements should continue to be directly assigned to the co-location arrangements.

bins.name: Preserve Co-Location Under Cost Causation
bins.desc: PSEG supports co-location arrangements as an efficient use of the transmission system and urges the Commission not to subject them to discriminatory study requirements or gross-load billing. Co-located loads should be treated the same as existing BTMG customers.
quotes: the Commission should provide data centers with the flexibility and optionality to select a configuration that best fits their particular circumstances and needs, including in front of the meter connections or co-location/behind the meter configurations.

applying such a study to co-located load arrangement would be unduly discriminatory under the FPA because it would treat co-located load arrangements differently than loads seeking to interconnect on a front of the meter basis.
it would be inappropriate to consider resource adequacy issues in the study process for co-located load/hybrid arrangements.

a transmission pricing methodology for a co-located arrangement that is based on gross load without any consideration for actual usage of the transmission system or the impact or use of the transmission system would clearly fail to comply with cost causation principles.
co-located load is using the transmission system in the same manner as BTMG customers and there would be no justification to treat behind the meter load different from BTMG.
network upgrades required by co-location load arrangements should continue to be directly assigned to the co-location arrangements.

bins.name: Act Now, End Uncertainty
bins.desc: PSEG emphasizes the need for the Commission to move quickly to establish final rules, citing two years of proceedings without action and the resulting uncertainty for developers, utilities, and investors.
quotes: the time has come for the Commission to act expeditiously by establishing rules that will provide clarity and finality.

bins.name: New Generation Must Keep Pace
bins.desc: PSEG describes a looming resource adequacy crisis in PJM, where aging thermal generation is retiring while load growth accelerates. It calls for an all-of-the-above strategy including improved load forecasting, state-sponsored generation, and an expedited interconnection track.
quotes: large loads should not create or exacerbate the resource adequacy problem that exists in many parts of the country.
it is necessary to implement an 'all-of-the-above' approach that includes bringing significant amounts of new generation supply on-line as soon as possible.
it would be inappropriate to consider resource adequacy issues in the study process for co-located load/hybrid arrangements.

bins.name: Protect Existing Customer Rates
bins.desc: PSEG repeatedly stresses that large load integration must not raise costs for existing customers. It links affordability directly to cost causation principles and efficient transmission planning.
quotes: large load connections should recognize and prioritize affordability for customers through efficient planning and use of the transmission system.

bins.name: Equal Treatment Under FPA
bins.desc: PSEG argues that co-located loads must not face rules more burdensome than those applied to front-of-meter loads or existing BTMG customers. Applying RMR studies or gross-load charges solely to co-located arrangements would constitute undue discrimination under the FPA.
quotes: applying such a study to co-located load arrangement would be unduly discriminatory under the FPA because it would treat co-located load arrangements differently than loads seeking to interconnect on a front of the meter basis.
layering on an RMR study that examines resource adequacy issues is unnecessary, inefficient, unduly discriminatory, and not the correct legal or practical fit for these arrangements.
co-located load is using the transmission system in the same manner as BTMG customers and there would be no justification to treat behind the meter load different from BTMG.

bins.name: Cost Causation Applies Throughout
bins.desc: PSEG invokes cost causation as the organizing principle for both transmission charges and upgrade cost allocation. It cites Commission precedent establishing that charges must track a customer's actual use and that system-wide upgrade benefits justify socialization while project-specific costs should be directly assigned.
quotes: transmission charges should be based on actual usage of the transmission system, guided by well-established cost causation principles.

a transmission pricing methodology for a co-located arrangement that is based on gross load without any consideration for actual usage of the transmission system or the impact or use of the transmission system would clearly fail to comply with cost causation principles.

bins.name: Two-Track Upgrade Cost Rule
bins.desc: PSEG proposes that front-of-meter upgrade costs be socialized across all transmission customers because those upgrades provide system-wide benefits, while co-located load upgrade costs be directly assigned to those arrangements because they do not use the transmission system for power delivery.
quotes: network upgrades to accommodate load interconnections in front of the meter should be allocated to all transmission customers, including large load additions.
network upgrades required by co-location load arrangements should continue to be directly assigned to the co-location arrangements.
